data.source: Assessment of Reading and Mathematics Development Study (ELEMENT)
data.notes: Large-scale assessment dataset; missing values handled with five multiple imputations. Students classified into bilingual/monolingual groups using parent and student questionnaires.

method.description: Secondary analyses of ELEMENT data examined whether speaking a minority language at home is associated with English as a foreign language achievement. Researchers created language-group indicators, imputed missing data, and ran cluster-robust multiple regressions that controlled for cognitive ability and socio-demographic factors.
steps: Obtain ELEMENT Grade-6 dataset including test scores and questionnaires.
Identify home-language status from parent reports; classify students into monolingual German or specific bilingual groups (Arabic-German, Chinese-German, Polish-German, Turkish-German, Other).
Compute outcome: English Cloze test weighted likelihood estimate (WLE, M=100, SD=20).

Prepare predictors: bilingual group dummies; German reading proficiency WLE; general cognitive ability (CFT 4-12R verbal & figural analogies); age; gender; socio-economic status (HISEI); parental education; cultural capital (books in home).
Impute missing data (five datasets) with a background model; combine results following Rubin’s rules.
Run linear regressions in Mplus 5.21 with class-level clustering (TYPE=COMPLEX) comparing successive models: uncontrolled; adding individual factors; adding family factors; adding German proficiency.
Interpret coefficients to evaluate advantages of bilingualism for English achievement.
method.models: Linear multiple regression with dummy variables; cluster-robust standard errors
method.outcome_variable: English foreign-language achievement (Cloze test WLE score)
method.independent_variables: Bilingual status or specific bilingual group indicators; German reading proficiency
method.control_variables: General cognitive ability, age, gender, HISEI, parental education, cultural capital
method.tools_software: Mplus 5.21 for regression; ConQuest for IRT scaling

results.summary: After controlling for cognitive and socio-economic background, bilingualism is positively related to English foreign-language achievement. The effect varies by language group: Polish-German, Chinese-German, and heterogeneous bilingual students outperform monolingual peers; Turkish-German students show a smaller positive effect; Arabic-German students do not differ. German reading proficiency is the strongest predictor of English scores. The final model explains 46% of the variance in English achievement.

numerical_results.outcome_name: German reading proficiency (standardized) effect on English (Model D)

metadata.original_paper_title: The effect of speaking a minority language at home on foreign language learning
